rulings.Case Title: JONATHAN W. WONG et al. v. Glassdoor, Inc.
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION TO COMPEL DOCUMENTS PURSUANT TO A SUBPOENA TO THIRD-PARTY GLASSDOOR, INC.
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, November 14, 2024, Line 1, PETITION TO COMPEL DOCUMENTS PURSUANT TO A SUBPOENA TO THIRD-PARTY GLASSDOOR, INC. Petitioners' "amended petition to compel documents pursuant to a subpoena to third-party Glassdoor, LLC" is granted. Petitioners are Chicago-area dentist Jonathan W. Wong and his LLC. Anonymous posters disparaged Dr. Wong on California-based Glassdoor's website; he sued in an Illinois court and served a subpoena on Glassdoor designed, inter alia, to identify the posters. Glassdoor refused to comply with the subpoena, occasioning this motion. Illinois substantive law applies. (Krinsky v. Doe 6 (2008) 159 Cal.App.4th 1154, 1173.) But the result would be the same under California law, where "constitutional protection is no longer available" when "criticism descends into defamation." (Yelp, Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (2017) 17 Cal.App.5th 1, 13 [cleaned up.) Here, the at-issue posts contain words imputing, at the least, employment-related sexual misconduct by Dr. Wong - words "defamatory per se" under Illinois law: e.g., "You know right away which girls have got on the 'good' side of the owner and perks available if you're willing to spend late nights alone with him;" "Owner only gives preferential treatment to female staff who go 'above and beyond' the job description;" "Dr. Wong has his assistants lie and con the other girls to do questionable things;" "the dentist industry's very own 'Mitch Kessler' (a philandering TV character); (Memo. 2:11-25); Van Horne v. Muller (1998) 185 Ill.2d 299, 307 (words imputing lacking integrity; words prejudicing an individual in his profession; words imputing fornication).) These posts are more than opinions, as Glassdoor argues. The petition is timely, as it regards a second subpoena. No sanctions are awarded. For the 9:30 a.m. Law & Motion calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 302 remotely. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: SHASE LELAND HERNANDEZ VS. SAN FRANCISCO CONSERVATORY OF MUSIC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Enforce Settlement Agreement As A Judgment [C.C.P. 664.6]
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, November 14, 2024, Line 2, 1- DEFENDANT SAN FRANCISCO CONSERVATORY OF MUSIC's Motion To Enforce Settlement Agreement As A Judgment [C.C.P. 664.6]. Defendant's motion to enforce the settlement agreement per CCP 664.6 is granted. On May 20, 2024, the mediator presented a "Mediator's Proposal" to the parties to resolve this case. (Vartain Decl., Ex. A.) The Mediator's Proposal contemplated settlement of the case on the following terms: (1) defendant's payment of $485,000 to plaintiff; (2) a mutual release; (3) 10% of the settlement designated as backpay (after deduction for attorney's fees and costs); and (4) subsequent execution of a written settlement. The parties subsequently became embroiled in a dispute regarding how to characterize the remaining 90% portion of the settlement and whether defendant may file an IRS Form 1099 with the IRS. To resolve this dispute, the parties entered into a joint stipulation for determination to the mediator. (Vartain Decl., B.) The stipulation was a writing signed by the parties and expressly noted that the Mediator's Proposal could be enforced per CCP 664.6. The parties therefore had an enforceable settlement. Under the parties' settlement, there is no basis to preclude defendant from filing the Form 1099 if it chooses to do so. First, caselaw indicates that such a proscription should be expressly stated in the settlement. (See Ward v. American Family Life Assur. Co. of Columbus (D.S.C. 2006) 444 F.Supp.2d 540, 542-544; Duse v. International Business Machines Corp. (2d Cir. 2001) 252 F.3d 151, 158 ["The language of the Settlement Agreement in the present case is unambiguous. The Agreement did not by its terms forbid IBM to file a Form 1099."].) Second, the stipulation asked the mediator to determine the Form 1099 issue and he responded that defendant was not prohibited from filing the form. (Vartain Decl., Ex. F.) Defendant is therefore entitled to entry of judgment in conformance with the parties' settlement, which does not prevent the filing of the Form 1099. For the 9:30 a.m. Law & Motion calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 302 remotely. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: ALLISON P. ROWE VS. GOLD DROP HOLDINGS, INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, November 14, 2024, Line 7, 1- DEFENDANTS GOLD DROP HOLDINGS, INC., SMD GLOBAL INC., JOSEPH ENCINOSA, CHRISTOPHER WYATT, AND LIAM WAITE'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Defendants' demurrer is overruled. Defendants say the complaint's Labor Code claims (counts 1-6) do not lie against individuals. (Memo. 5:11-13.) But Labor Code 581.1 provides that these particular claims lie against any "natural person who is an owner, director, officer, or managing agent of the employer" - which the individual defendants are pled to be. (Cmplt. 3:21-4:3.) The cases defendants rely on pre-date Labor Code 581.1. Whether unjust enrichment (count 9) is a cause of action is unsettled, but of no moment; any "double relief" issues can be addressed at trial. The elements of contract breach (count 10) are adequately alleged (Cmplt. 21:21-24:4); plaintiff need not "prove" her claim (Memo. 7:5) at the pleading stage. As to conversion (count 11), a complaint may plead alternative legal theories. (Adams v. Paul (1995) 11 Cal.4th 583, 593.) rulings.Case Title: ALLISON P. ROWE VS. GOLD DROP HOLDINGS, INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, November 14, 2024, Line 7, 2- DEFENDANTS GOLD DROP HOLDINGS, INC., AND SMD GLOBAL INC.'S MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT. Defendants' motion to strike is denied. The motion is limited to Penal Code violations. (Not. 2:9-12; Cmplt. 19:20-20:7.) A criminal violation is a proper "unlawful" UCL predicate. A judge's "passions" are unlikely to be stirred (Memo. 2:15) and a jury typically does not see the complaint.
